explanation: When prop_0 occurs, it must be the case that prop_1 already occurred and that this happened before any prop_2; alternatively, a common interpretation is that between any prop_0 and the next prop_2, prop_1 must occur before prop_2. However, the pattern '(when p, q before r)' is usually interpreted as: whenever p holds, then before the first future occurrence of r (if it ever occurs), q must happen. In LTL, this is coded as: whenever p occurs, either r never occurs, or q occurs before r occurs.

The requirement is: whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 must occur before prop_2. This means that after any point where prop_0 holds, it must be the case that if prop_2 ever occurs, then prop_1 must have occurred strictly before it ('before' is traditionally interpreted as strict precedence). Using LTL, this is G(prop_0 -> (!prop_2 U (prop_1 | prop_2 & (!prop_1 U prop_1)))). However, a more standard way is: whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must occur before prop_2 (if prop_2 occurs at all after prop_0). This can be captured as: G(prop_0 -> ((!prop_2) U prop_1) | G(!prop_2)), meaning : on every occurrence of prop_0, either prop_1 happens before prop_2, or prop_2 never happens after prop_0.

The requirement states: 'When prop_0, the prop_1 before prop_2'. This means: whenever prop_0 holds, at some future point prop_1 must occur before prop_2. In LTL, this is captured by: whenever prop_0 is true, then (from that point on) prop_2 cannot happen until prop_1 happens ('!prop_2 U prop_1'), i.e., we express that if prop_0 becomes true, then prop_2 cannot happen before prop_1 has occurred.

There is no 'previous' operator in standard LTL. To express that whenever prop_0 occurs, it was immediately preceded by prop_1, we rephrase it: prop_0 cannot occur at a moment when prop_1 was not true in the immediately previous step. In LTL, we can write this as: it is always the case that if prop_0 occurs, then in the previous step, prop_1 was true. This can be encoded as: it is never the case that prop_0 occurs at position 0 (the first state), and everywhere else, we always have that if prop_0 occurs, then previously prop_1 was true. In standard future LTL, we can express this using the "until" operator: for all paths, it's not possible for prop_0 to happen without being immediately preceded by prop_1. This can be modeled as: it is always the case that if prop_0 occurs, then either it is the very first state (special handling, but out of expressivity), or prop_1 was true just before. In standard LTL, we can only encode the following: whenever prop_0 holds, it was preceded at some point by prop_1. However, for the immediate predecessor, we can't do it exactly in future-only LTL. Thus, an over-approximation is: globally, if prop_0 holds, then previously, prop_1 held at some point (not necessarily immediately before), i.e. G(prop_0 -> P prop_1). But 'P' is not available.
Thus, within standard LTL:
An approximation: globally, it's not possible for prop_0 to happen without first seeing prop_1, i.e. (!prop_1) U prop_0 is false. Or: it is always the case that if prop_0 occurs, then previously, prop_1 occurred at some point (but not necessarily immediately before).

However, if we consider only future LTL operators and no previous-state, the best we can do is:
- 'prop_1' must be true at any step immediately before 'prop_0' becomes true.
- One way: globally, if NOT prop_1, then X NOT prop_0. i.e., you cannot go from not-prop_1 to prop_0 in one step.

That is, if prop_1 is false, then prop_0 cannot occur in the next step: G((!prop_1) -> X(!prop_0))

This requirement states that whenever prop_0 happens, prop_1 must occur before prop_2. In other words, on any point where prop_0 holds, starting from that point onwards, it must be the case that prop_2 does not happen until prop_1 happens (i.e., prop_1 precedes prop_2 after prop_0).

The requirement says: whenever 'prop_0' occurs, 'prop_1' must happen before 'prop_2' does. This means, between the current point and the first occurrence of 'prop_2', if 'prop_0' holds, then 'prop_1' must occur at some point before 'prop_2'. In LTL, this is typically expressed by ensuring that if 'prop_2' has not occurred yet, and 'prop_0' occurs now, then before the next 'prop_2', 'prop_1' must occur.

This requirement specifies that whenever prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must have occurred before prop_2 occurs. In LTL, this can be captured by globally requiring that if prop_0 holds, then until prop_2 occurs, prop_1 must occur at some point before prop_2. That is, in every occurrence where prop_0 holds, if prop_2 is not yet true, prop_1 must become true before prop_2.

This states that whenever prop_0 occurs, prop_1 must have occurred at some time before the next occurrence of prop_2 (assuming 'before' means strictly before in the past within the same run). In LTL, we express this as: whenever prop_0 occurs, in the past (before prop_0) there was a prop_1 that occurred, and there has been no occurrence of prop_2 since that prop_1 up to the prop_0.

The specification says that whenever prop_0 is true, then prop_1 must occur before prop_2. This means that from every point where prop_0 holds, until prop_2 happens, if prop_2 has not happened yet, then prop_1 must happen at least once before that. In LTL, this is expressed as: globally, if prop_0 holds, then either prop_2 will never happen (so it's vacuously satisfied), or if prop_2 happens, then before the first time prop_2 happens, prop_1 has already happened.

The statement says: When prop_0, the prop_1 before prop_2. This means that whenever prop_0 occurs, it should not be the case that prop_2 has happened before prop_1. In LTL, this is encoded by saying: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must be true at some point before (or at the same time as) the first occurrence of prop_2, or if prop_2 never occurs, prop_1 must eventually occur. This is done by stating: globally, if prop_0 becomes true, then either prop_1 holds until prop_2 (including if prop_2 never happens), or prop_1 already holds. That is: G (prop_0 -> (!prop_2 U prop_1)).
